speaker: interviewer
text: Hi. On behalf of the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I'd like to welcome Irene Zisblatt. It's October 21, 1995, in Pembroke Pines, Florida. And we'll be conducting the interview in English. My name is Jennifer Resnick. Thank you. Irene Zisblatt, take one. Irene, could you tell us your name, your maiden name, the date of your birth, and the town you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Irene Zisblatt, and my maiden name is Irene Zeigelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's spelled Z-E-I-G-E-L-S-T-E-I-N. And I was born December 28, 1930, in a small town in the Carpathian Mountains called Poleno. It's spelled P-O-L-E-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your mother and father's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rochel, and my father's name was Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was Rochel Merlmestin. That's M-E-R-L-S-T-I-N-- L-M-E-S-T-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your family? Your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- we were six-- one sister and four brothers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my oldest brother-- I was the oldest in the family, and my older brother after me, his name was Mendel, and he 11 years old. He was two years younger than me. Then I had a brother named Meyer, and he was two years younger than my oldest brother. Then I had a brother, David. And he was about four years younger than my second brother. Then I had a sister, and she was about two years younger than David. And then I had another brother-- two and a half-- and he was about a year and a half or two years younger than my sister. And her name was Hindu. And my youngest brother's name was Luzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, could you tell us about your family life, growing up in Poleno?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life was very happy. We were bought up very orthodox. My grandparents were alive most of my growing up life. We were together a lot, and they taught us a lot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to public school, and I went to Jewish school, which we called the cheder. We had one temple in town, where all the Jews went to the same temple. We were orthodox. Everybody was orthodox, so the men and women were separated during services. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were about, I would say, about 60 or 70 Jewish families in town. So we were all very close. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a lot of non-Jewish people living, of course. There was a church in town. There was a city hall. And unfortunately, we had a railroad. So it made it easier for the Nazis to get us out of there to the concentration camp. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe Poleno? What kind of city or town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a town. It was-- it consisted of one main street and-- well, actually, two main streets. One was going across town, and one was leading out of town to-- excuse me. They were all leaving out of town, but in different directions. And then there were a couple of side streets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not like the streets here. More like alleys that you would see here. But those were streets over there. And most of the people in town were-- they either had grocery stores or farms. People were working for one another. There was-- Poleno was a resort town, and there was a resort place, and it was called the Polenoi Baths, [HUNGARIAN]. In Hungarian, it was [HUNGARIAN], so it was baths. The reason is because the area where the hotels were, where the resort was, the grounds had 13 different mineral waters in there. Only two of them were used, one for drinking, and one for the baths. And that place had that water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people used to come from all over the country and from out of the country to take these baths. They were hot mineral baths for-- as close as I can remember-- for rheumatism and muscular-- muscular purposes. Other than that, people used to come for vacation. And there were four-story-- two big buildings, about four stories, two hotels. And then the rest of the buildings were like motel-style, one floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two bars in there, and two kitchens, one kosher kitchen, and one non-kosher kitchen. Restaurant. Dining rooms. And it was beautiful. It was all-- it was-- it was flowers and pine trees, and then there was woods, where the children of the guests used to go horseback riding. And it was just-- it was a beautiful mountain resort. And my father and his cousins owned and worked the place. So they were always very busy. In the wintertime, when it was very cold and snowy, the place was closed for vacation. But they used to donate the place for orphanage children, and children that were handicapped that needed the baths. So those-- that's what it was open for, just for those purposes. And people from town used to come and take their drinking water from there, because it was healthy. And I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this open to Jewish and Christian people from the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was open for everybody. There was-- there was no restrictions whatsoever. Even gypsies. So we had a lot of gypsies in Hungary. And-- and I went to school. Unfortunately, my education was cut when I was nine years old. We-- we started to feel the pressure of the war. Of course, I didn't understand it at the time, why we couldn't go to school. But I couldn't go to school because I was Jewish. Everybody else went to school. And all my other Jewish friends and family and cousins, none of us were allowed to go to school from them on in. So we just went to Jewish school, to cheder, until we were taken away to the ghetto and then to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with your time once you weren't in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In elementary school?

speaker: interviewer
text: Once elementary school ceased to exist for the Jewish children, what did the children do during the day? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we wen to cheder for longer periods of time after that. And then we-- we just stayed home and did whatever our parents asked us to do at home. And of course, a lot of us had hobbies, so we devoted a little time. And we studied on our own as much as we could, because of course our parents, they-- they taught us whatever they thought we needed to know, but without school, it was kind of hard for them too. So everybody did the best they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language you spoke at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke Yiddish at home. And my parents and grandparents, if they didn't want us to understand, until we learned Hungarian, they spoke Hungarian, because they really came from the old Hungarian. And then also we spoke Czech for a while, because I went to Czech kindergarten and also to first grade. So we had a lot of people that moved into our town that were professionals, like gendarmes-- Czech gendarmes-- people that worked in City Hall. They were Czechs. So there was a Czech school.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time before '39, did you experience anti-Semitism in Poleno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vicious. I didn't understand it. But I-- I remember hearing, you know, that there were certain people that believed in communism, and they were bad, and they did bad things to Jews, and-- but I really never really saw it or understood it, but-- until 1939. Then I really felt and saw the pressure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your friends? Did you have friends that were only Jewish, or were you friendly with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a lot of non-Jewish friends, and we were really very, very close. We shared things together. We did-- until the war. And then it was-- it was awful. Then it was-- it was such a disappointment in my life. My-- my-- my life just shattered, to see my friends that I shared everything with being so hostile and so mean and so anti-Semitic towards me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were calling me names. They wouldn't allow-- they didn't allow me to go to their homes anymore. They didn't-- they were taking things from me to make me upset. They-- they did all this, you know, showing me how-- how they don't like me anymore. And I don't know whether they were coached by their parents. And that was in '39 and '40, '41. And all this pressure I felt from different-- different. So the Jewish children-- I, for one, was stuck to home, because I was so disappointed in-- in-- in the outside closeness with people, that I-- whatever I could do, I just did at home. And I used love to write, so I used to write poems and-- and-- and songs, and and all that stuff. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your grandparents also lived in Poleno. Can you describe your Jewish family life for Friday night? Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My father's parents lived in-- we had a duplex house, so they lived on the other side of the house, and we celebrated Shabbat and all holidays together. On a Friday, it was the busiest day of the week, because even my-- my mother's mother, which lived in the next town, used to come early Friday morning and woke everybody up at 5:00 in the morning and-- to bake challah and bread and rolls for the week. For the whole week, we did on Friday. And I was eight years old, and I had to get up at 5 o'clock in the morning. I was still going to school. The Jewish children were not restricted from school yet. And she woke me up at 5 o'clock in the morning, and she taught me how to make matzo for the soup, and to braid the challah for Shabbat. And I was very angry with her, because I wanted to sleep at 5 o'clock in the morning. But today, I am very grateful that she did that. And so we were-- I was up at 5:00, and my grandmother taught me whatever she felt that I should know at that time. And I had this constant argument with her. But I don't have to do this at 5 o'clock in the morning. And she says, you have to do that, because when you get married, you're going to have to do that for your family. And I used to say to her, but I'm going to get somebody to do that for me. And she used to say, but you're going to have to know how to do it in order to show the other person how to do it, whoever is going to do it for you. And today, I remember those words, and they're so true. And so we were busy all morning, baking and preparing for Shabbat. Of course, I went to school, and by the time I came back, it was time to make the cholent. And the cholent was something that my father and my grandfather and my grandmother worked together, because each one liked different things in the cholent. My father liked corn. So he roasted the corn in the oven after my grandmother finished baking. And then he chopped it in one of those old-fashioned-- I don't know what they call it. But in Jewish, they call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. They-- they-- they, like, kind of pounded it to pieces. So my father did that. And my grandfather liked barley, So he used to pick out the bad barley from the barley to prepare that. And of course, my grandmother did the rest of the stuff. And when I came home from school, I-- I was carrying the pot to the bakery, because that's where the oven was, and everybody's pot went into that oven for Shabbat. So all night long, it was cooking for Shabbat dinner. And so that was done. And then-- and then I helped my grandmother and my mother prepare candle lighting and Shabbat services for Friday night. And before it-- before candle light, I used to carry my father's and my grandfather's tallit to the-- to the temple, because they weren't allowed to carry. And I wasn't bat mitzvahed yet, so I was allowed to carry. So I did that. And when they come home-- when they came home from services, we had Shabbat dinner, and the traditional chicken soup, with the noodles in the soup, and chicken. We didn't have too much chicken. But it was-- it was delicious. And apple sauce from our own apples, our own apple trees. And-- and it was just wonderful. And that was our Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents stayed over?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents lived there. My grandparents lived in the same house, but they had their own-- it was a duplex. So their entrance was on the other side. But we had a door from our house to go into our grandparents' house, through I think it was the kitchen and one of their rooms that connected. So we didn't have to go outside to go to their house, but we just went through from one house to the other. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and our life, other than that, my grandfather was-- he kept us really busy, because he had a farm. And so on our home grounds, we had three cows, and we had a goat, and we had chickens, and geese. And he-- that was-- you know. I guess we lived off of that stuff. He had a beautiful, beautiful vegetable garden, with tomatoes and string beans and all that stuff, in the backyard. We had a big backyard. And of course he grew spruce treats-- trees, and they were lined up along our property by the fence. And there were little ones and big ones. They always looked so beautiful. And at Christmas time, I wanted to have a Christmas tree. I wanted one of those trees to-- because all my non-Jewish friends had. They were so busy, you know, with Christmas, and they were wrapping their own candies with special papers, and it just looked like a lot of fun. And I was helping doing their candy, and I wanted some of my own, but my grandfather said, you're not allowed to have-- we don't celebrate Christmas, and we're not allowed to have one in the house. But I had an idea. And I-- we had a sukkah that was attached to the house. And at-- at sukkahs, the roof opened up. And when it wasn't sukkah, it was inside. So I said to my grandfather, why don't we open up the roof and then put the Christmas tree in there, and then we'll have-- it will be outside. It wouldn't be in the house. And he said, no, no. It's too cold. It's too cold. We can't do that. You can pick out any one you like, and that will be yours, but it's got to stay there. So I picked out one that grew in the garden. And the glitters from the snow, I made believe it was the decorations, and that was my Christmas tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your younger brothers and sisters? Were you very close? Were you very involved with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- yes. We did everything together. We-- see, we all went to school together because the school was a two-room building. So they combined all the classes, like maybe four grades in one room. And so we all went to school at the same time, only maybe the little ones went to this room, and the older ones went to that. But we were like two, three classes combined together in the school. And so we went to school together, and we did, of course, everything together. They had more responsibility as far as the education, because they were boys. And they-- in a Jewish family, the boys have to be very educated in Judaism. So they went to cheder every day after school. And so they were a little more busy than the girls, because it wasn't as important for the girls to have the education that the boys. But yes, we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first-- first begin to understand that there were changes happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to school, and they told me that I have to go home. I am no longer allowed because I am a Jew. And I came home crying, and my father was-- already had notices from-- from his business also that there was pressure there. So he already knew that we weren't allowed to school. And he-- he said, don't worry. We'll fix it. It will be OK. Don't get upset. We will make it OK so you can go back to school. And-- or to another school. And-- but it never happened. They couldn't fix anything. But the pressures were-- From then on in, they were-- we noticed the youth, like the older youth, they were called the [NON-ENGLISH]. That was Neo-Nazi groups. They-- and they were seeking out Jews on the way to work or on the way home from synagogue, and they were beating them up. They were destroying homes. They were destroying synagogues. They were pulling out scriptures and burning the Torahs and the [INAUDIBLE]. Because everybody that went to-- all the men, they-- they-- normally, they leave everything in the temple for the next day. And then they started to bring everything home, because they were burning synagogues in the areas. So in order to preserve their-- their possessions, they brought them home after each service. But then they got to it anyway. And they-- and there was a lot of-- a lot of beating up people. And one night, I remember, my father had a group of people, and they got together every-- once a week, and they played cards. And they loved to play the violin. So they either practiced the violin, and they played cards. And there were-- there were about six or eight men. And one night, they were playing cards next door, in my-- in our neighbor's house. And where I slept, my window was facing that yard, and I can see that house from my window, from my bedroom. In fact, I went to kindergarten in that house, because one side of the house was made into a-- it was one room, and there was a kindergarten there. And in the middle of the night, I was woken up by a terrible noise. So I got up, and I looked out the window, and I saw a bunch of people were with-- I couldn't make out whether it was-- like, rifles or just big pieces of wood. And they were beating somebody up. And then I remembered that my father was there, and his brother and a cousin, and then their friends. They were in that same house. But I saw a lot of people running away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, just then, I was going to start either screaming or waking somebody up, I heard somebody coming in through the door. And my father and my uncle came home, and they bolted the door. And I ran out from my room, and I was scared, and I said, what is all that noise? And my father said, shh, go back to sleep and be very quiet. It's OK. So I went back to sleep. But then the next day, two people from that group, we never saw again. And then-- then they found-- you know, they killed them. And nobody took the blame for it. Nobody was punished for it. And they just-- from then on in, everybody was very careful not to let the children by themselves to go anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: We never went into the woods. We used to go to the woods to pick, you know, blackberries and raspberries and mushrooms and all that. I used to go with my grandfather to pick mushrooms so he could show me which ones are the good ones and which ones are the bad ones. And we never did that again. And we just stayed very close to home most of the time. And at night, sometimes my mother would be afraid to put the lights on, so they couldn't see who was inside. And it was really very hard from then on in. But then my father got involved with a group of people, and they were helping people to get to Israel. They were a group of-- I don't-- I don't think they had a name. They were just doing these things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, we heard rumors that came from Poland, what they were doing to the Jews in Poland. And of course, nobody believed it. But then the people-- some of the people were able to escape. And we were close to the border, so some people were-- managed-- you know, made it across the border and came to us. And my father and other people in the congregation, they would help them to get, like, to the next town and to the next town. And there were other people that intercepted them to help them to get to Palestine. Whether they ever got there, I don't know. But I just remember that people used to come and spend the night in our house. And then the next night, they would take them somewhere else. And then maybe other people would come. Like, one or two people at time. And one night, I overheard a conversation. One man was spending the night, and he was talking to my father, and he was telling my father that the-- the Nazis. And I didn't know who they were or who he was talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in like '41 or '42. The Nazis were taking the Jewish children to Dniester. And I remember Dniester, in geography, was a river in the Ukraine or in Poland. Somewhere in there. I couldn't remember where. But I remember the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said that they were tearing the children in half and throwing them in Dniester. And  I had a nightmare. And the next day, the man was gone. He was no longer in our house, so I couldn't ask him. But I said to my father, why are they throwing the children-- what do you mean they're tearing them in half, and they're throwing them in Dniester? Why? And he said, you never heard that. That never happened. And you have to forget about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but that man said, that was here last night. He said, there was no man here. And you are not to tell anybody there was  a man or what he said. So I realized that something terrible is going on that they don't-- they don't want the children to know. So I just kept quiet about it and never, never mentioned it again to anybody.
